medications.indication_rationale: Low-dose doxycycline for its anti-inflammatory effect in papulopustular rosacea (not as an antibacterial).
medications.dose_route_frequency: e.g., 40-50 mg daily (as per source note; per prescriber)

medications.drug_name: topical rosacea agent (metronidazole / ivermectin / azelaic acid)
medications.indication_rationale: Topical anti-inflammatory/antiparasitic agents for rosacea, added at review.
These damage the skin barrier and aggravate rosacea — must be stopped.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop harsh scrubs/acid toners, switch to a gentle non-foaming cleanser + plain moisturiser, use physical sun protection (hat + gentle SPF), and avoid triggers (sun, alcohol, heat); cool compresses for burning.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Repairs the barrier and prevents trigger-driven flares.

patient_education_points.point: This is a rosacea flare, not a facial infection — the pustules are sterile (no bacteria) and there are no blackheads. The low-dose antibiotic is used for its anti-inflammatory effect, not to kill germs.
Stop the scrubs/acid toners, use gentle skincare and sun protection, and avoid your triggers (sun, alcohol, heat). The burning eases in days; the bumps take 2–4 weeks to settle.
If your face becomes hot, hard and swollen with a fever, the redness spreads to your eyelids, or your eyes become red, painful or gritty — seek medical/eye care.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Response to doxycycline and add topical therapy; reinforce trigger/skincare advice; assess for ocular rosacea.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Facial cellulitis or ocular rosacea features — urgent/eye care.

must_include_items: Diagnose papulopustular rosacea (no comedones) and screen for facial cellulitis and ocular rosacea
Stop harsh skincare; gentle regimen + sun protection + trigger avoidance
errors_of_commission: Treating as facial cellulitis (ED/full-dose antibiotics) without red flags
Treating as acne / prescribing comedonal therapy
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires rosacea diagnosis (vs cellulitis/acne), anti-inflammatory therapy + skincare/trigger advice, and a cellulitis/ocular safety-net.
